title: Learn Trading
subtitle: Everything you need to know to start trading Bitcoin, from zero to confident. Built from expert guides, community wisdom, and professional best practices.

remember.title: REMEMBER
remember.rule1: 1. Never invest more than you can afford to lose
remember.rule2: 2. Always use a stop-loss on every trade
remember.rule3: 3. Start small — learn with amounts that don't cause stress
remember.rule4: 4. Paper trade first — practice without real money until consistent
remember.rule5: 5. The market will always be there tomorrow — no trade is worth your mental health

whatIsBitcoin.title: What is Bitcoin?
whatIsBitcoin.p1: Bitcoin is a decentralized digital currency that operates without a central bank or single administrator. It was created in 2009 by an anonymous person/group known as Satoshi Nakamoto.
whatIsBitcoin.p2: Key properties: Fixed supply (21 million max), decentralized (no single point of control), transparent (all transactions on public blockchain).

howTrading.title: How Does Trading Work?
howTrading.p1: Trading means buying and selling Bitcoin to profit from price changes. You buy when you think the price will go up, and sell when you think it will go down.
howTrading.spot: Spot trading: Buy/sell actual Bitcoin. You own the asset.
howTrading.futures: Futures/Derivatives: Trade contracts that track Bitcoin's price. Can use leverage (borrowed money) to amplify gains — and losses.
howTrading.longShort: Long: Betting price goes UP. Short: Betting price goes DOWN.

whatMoves.title: What Moves Bitcoin's Price?
whatMoves.supply: Supply & Demand: More buyers than sellers = price up. More sellers = price down.
whatMoves.halvings: Halvings: Every ~4 years, new BTC created per block is cut in half. Reduces supply, historically bullish.
whatMoves.macro: Macro events: Interest rates, inflation, dollar strength (DXY), geopolitical events.
whatMoves.sentiment: Sentiment: News, social media, fear & greed. Markets are driven by human emotion.
whatMoves.whales: Whale activity: Large holders (1000+ BTC) moving coins to/from exchanges can signal buying or selling.

marketCap.title: What is Market Cap?
marketCap.p1: Market Cap = Price x Circulating Supply. It measures the total value of all coins in circulation.
marketCap.p2: BTC at $97,000 with 19.8M coins = ~$1.92 trillion market cap.
marketCap.p3: Higher market cap = more established, lower volatility. Lower market cap = more speculative, higher risk/reward.

wallet.title: What is a Wallet?
wallet.p1: A crypto wallet stores your private keys — the passwords that prove you own your Bitcoin.
wallet.hot: Hot wallet: Connected to internet (exchange accounts, mobile apps). Convenient but less secure.
wallet.cold: Cold wallet: Offline hardware devices (Ledger, Trezor). Most secure for large holdings.
wallet.warning: "Not your keys, not your coins" — if the exchange gets hacked, you lose your funds. Self-custody is safest for large amounts.

market.title: Market Order
market.p1: Buys or sells immediately at the current market price.
market.cons: Cons: You may get a slightly different price than expected (slippage), especially in volatile markets.
market.useWhen: Use when: You need to enter/exit NOW and price precision doesn't matter much.

limit.title: Limit Order
limit.p1: Sets a specific price at which you want to buy or sell. Only executes if the market reaches that price.
limit.pros: Pros: You control the exact price. No slippage.
limit.cons: Cons: May never fill if price doesn't reach your level.
limit.useWhen: Use when: You have a specific target price and can wait for it.

stopLoss.title: Stop-Loss Order
stopLoss.p1: Automatically sells when price drops to a set level, limiting your loss.
stopLoss.example: Example: Buy at $97,000, set stop-loss at $95,000. If price drops to $95K, it auto-sells. Max loss = $2,000 per BTC.
stopLoss.useWhen: Use when: ALWAYS. Every trade should have a stop-loss. No exceptions.

takeProfit.title: Take-Profit Order
takeProfit.p1: Automatically sells when price rises to your target, locking in profit.
takeProfit.example: Example: Buy at $97,000, set take-profit at $103,000. Locks in $6,000 profit per BTC.
takeProfit.useWhen: Use when: Always set alongside stop-loss. Have BOTH exit points before entering.

stopLimit.title: Stop-Limit Order
stopLimit.p1: Combines stop and limit: when price hits the stop price, a limit order is placed (not a market order).
stopLimit.pros: Pros: More price control than a regular stop-loss.
stopLimit.cons: Cons: In fast crashes, the limit order may not fill, leaving you exposed.

trailingStop.title: Trailing Stop
trailingStop.p1: A stop-loss that moves up with the price. Locks in profit as price rises, triggers if price drops by a set amount.
trailingStop.example: Example: Set 3% trailing stop. Price rises from $97K to $103K, stop follows to $99,910. If price drops 3%, it sells at ~$99,910.
trailingStop.useWhen: Use when: You're in a winning trade and want to ride the trend while protecting gains.

rsi.name: RSI (Relative Strength Index)
rsi.desc: Measures momentum on a 0-100 scale. Above 70 = overbought (potential drop). Below 30 = oversold (potential bounce). Most reliable on higher timeframes (4h, 1D).
rsi.tip: Don't buy just because RSI is low. Wait for RSI to turn UP from oversold.

macd.name: MACD
macd.desc: Shows trend direction and momentum. The MACD line crossing above the signal line = bullish. Crossing below = bearish. Histogram shows the gap between the two lines.
macd.tip: MACD + RSI together is the most popular combo. MACD for trend, RSI for timing.

movingAverages.name: Moving Averages (EMA/SMA)
movingAverages.desc: Smooth out price data to show trends. EMA 9 & 21 for short-term, EMA 50 & 200 for long-term. Price above the average = bullish trend.
movingAverages.tip: Golden Cross (50 crosses above 200) = very bullish. Death Cross (50 below 200) = bearish.

bollinger.name: Bollinger Bands
bollinger.desc: Shows volatility range. Price near upper band = potentially overbought. Near lower band = oversold. Bands narrowing = big move coming (breakout).
bollinger.tip: Best used WITH volume. Breakout + high volume = real move. Low volume = likely fakeout.

volume.name: Volume
volume.desc: Number of coins traded. High volume = strong conviction. Low volume = weak move. Volume precedes price — watch for volume spikes before big moves.
volume.tip: Price up + volume up = strong trend. Price up + volume down = weakening, potential reversal.

supportResistance.name: Support & Resistance
supportResistance.desc: Price levels where buying (support) or selling (resistance) pressure is strong. Price bounces off these levels repeatedly.
supportResistance.tip: Once support breaks, it becomes resistance (and vice versa). These are the most important levels to watch.

fibonacci.name: Fibonacci Retracement
fibonacci.desc: Key levels (23.6%, 38.2%, 50%, 61.8%) where price often bounces during pullbacks. Based on the golden ratio.
fibonacci.tip: The 0.618 level is the most important. If price holds there during a pullback, the trend is likely intact.

ichimoku.name: Ichimoku Cloud
ichimoku.desc: All-in-one indicator showing support/resistance, trend direction, and momentum. Price above cloud = bullish. Below = bearish.
ichimoku.tip: The cloud itself acts as support/resistance. Thick cloud = strong. Thin cloud = weak, breakout likely.

fearGreed.name: Fear & Greed Index
fearGreed.desc: Market sentiment score 0-100. Extreme Fear (0-25) = potential buy. Extreme Greed (75-100) = potential sell. Uses volatility, volume, social media, and dominance.
fearGreed.tip: "Be fearful when others are greedy, greedy when others are fearful" — Warren Buffett.

fundingRate.name: Funding Rate
fundingRate.desc: Shows if the derivatives market is over-leveraged long or short. High positive = too many longs (bearish signal). Negative = too many shorts (bullish signal).
fundingRate.tip: Extreme funding rates often precede liquidation cascades and sharp reversals.

hodl.title: HODLing (Buy & Hold)
hodl.p1: The simplest strategy. Buy Bitcoin and hold for years regardless of short-term price swings.
hodl.bestFor: Best for: Beginners. Historically, anyone who held BTC for 4+ years has been profitable.
hodl.risk: Risk: Low (if you can handle seeing -50% drops without panic selling).

dca.title: Dollar Cost Averaging (DCA)
dca.p1: Invest a fixed amount at regular intervals (weekly/monthly) regardless of price. Removes the stress of timing the market.
dca.bestFor: Best for: Beginners and long-term investors. Proven to outperform most timing strategies.
dca.example: Example: $100/week for 2 years. You buy more BTC when cheap, less when expensive. Average cost smooths out.

swing.title: Swing Trading
swing.p1: Hold positions for days to weeks, capturing medium-term price swings using technical analysis.
swing.bestFor: Best for: Intermediate traders. Requires understanding of charts, indicators, and risk management.
swing.tools: Key tools: Support/resistance, RSI, MACD, volume. Enter at support, exit at resistance.

day.title: Day Trading
day.p1: Open and close positions within the same day. Requires constant monitoring and fast execution.
day.bestFor: Best for: Advanced traders only. 95% of day traders lose money — this is a statistical fact.
day.warning: Warning: High stress, high fees, requires discipline. Start with paper trading (practice with fake money).

onePercent.title: The 1% Risk Rule
onePercent.p1: Never risk more than 1% of your total portfolio on a single trade. This is THE most important rule in trading.
onePercent.example: Example: $10,000 account = max $100 risk per trade. If your stop-loss is 2% below entry, your position size = $5,000.
onePercent.p2: With the 1% rule, you can lose 10 trades in a row and only be down 10%. Without it, one bad trade can destroy your account.

srTrading.title: Support/Resistance Trading
srTrading.p1: Identify key price levels where BTC repeatedly bounces (support) or gets rejected (resistance).
srTrading.strategy: Strategy: Buy near support with stop-loss just below. Sell near resistance or set take-profit there.
srTrading.confirmation: Confirmation: Wait for a bounce (don't buy into a falling knife). Volume + RSI turning up = stronger signal.

onePercent.title: The 1% Rule (Expanded)
onePercent.p1: Never risk more than 1% of your total account on any single trade. This is the foundation of survival in trading.
onePercent.formula: Formula: Max Risk = Account Size x 0.01
onePercent.tip: Even 10 consecutive losses = only -10%. You survive. Without the 1% rule, one bad trade can end your career.

riskReward.title: Risk-to-Reward Ratio
riskReward.p1: The ratio between how much you risk (stop-loss distance) and how much you stand to gain (take-profit distance).
riskReward.minimum: Minimum target: 1:2 — risk $1 to make $2. This means you only need to win 34% of trades to be profitable.
riskReward.tip: Never take a trade with R:R below 1:1.5. If the setup doesn't offer good R:R, skip it — there will always be another trade.

stopLoss.title: Stop-Loss Placement
stopLoss.p1: Where you place your stop-loss matters more than whether you use one. A badly placed stop gets hit by normal market noise.
stopLoss.tip: A good stop-loss is placed where your trade thesis is invalidated — if price reaches that level, your reason for entering no longer holds.

positionSizing.title: Position Sizing
positionSizing.p1: The exact formula to calculate how big your position should be:
positionSizing.formula: Position Size = Risk Amount / (Entry Price - Stop-Loss Price)

portfolioHeat.title: Portfolio Heat
portfolioHeat.p1: Portfolio heat = the total risk across ALL your open positions combined.
portfolioHeat.max: Max recommended: 6% total portfolio risk at any time.
portfolioHeat.tip: Most blown accounts come from having too many positions open at once during a market crash. Portfolio heat is your defense.

leverage.title: Leverage Dangers
leverage.p1: Leverage amplifies both gains AND losses. Here's the math most people ignore:
leverage.safe: Safe leverage: 2-5x maximum, with tight stop-losses. Most professional traders use 1-3x. If you need high leverage to make money, your position sizing is wrong.

drawdown.title: Drawdown Recovery
drawdown.p1: Losses are not symmetrical. Recovering from a loss requires a larger percentage gain than the percentage you lost:
drawdown.tip: This is why protecting capital is more important than making gains. A 50% loss requires a 100% gain just to break even. Prevention > cure.

fomo.title: FOMO (Fear of Missing Out)
fomo.p1: That painful feeling when price is pumping and you're not in the trade. Your brain screams "BUY NOW before it goes higher!"
fomo.tip: The market is open 24/7, 365 days a year. There is ALWAYS another opportunity. Missing one trade is nothing — buying the top is everything.

revenge.title: Revenge Trading
revenge.p1: You just lost money. You feel angry, frustrated, humiliated. Your instinct is to immediately open a bigger trade to "win it back."
revenge.tip: Professional traders have a rule: after 2-3 consecutive losses, stop trading for the day. The market will be there tomorrow. Your capital might not be.

confirmation.title: Confirmation Bias
confirmation.p1: When you have a position, your brain automatically filters information to support your view and ignores evidence against it.
confirmation.tip: The best traders are always looking for reasons they might be wrong. The worst traders only look for reasons they're right.

overtrading.title: Overtrading
overtrading.p1: More trades does NOT mean more profit. Each trade has fees, slippage, and emotional cost. Most traders would be more profitable making fewer, higher-quality trades.

tradingPlan.title: The Trading Plan
tradingPlan.p1: Write your plan BEFORE you enter the trade, while you're thinking clearly. Once money is on the line, emotions take over.
tradingPlan.tip: If you can't write a plan for a trade, don't take the trade. "I just feel like it'll go up" is not a plan.

detachment.title: Emotional Detachment
detachment.p1: Every trade is a probability, not a certainty. Even the best setups fail 30-40% of the time. Treating trades as statistics instead of personal wins/losses is the key to longevity.
detachment.tip: Think of yourself as a casino, not a gambler. The casino doesn't celebrate every hand — it trusts the math over thousands of hands.

whenNot.title: When NOT to Trade
whenNot.p1: Some of the best trades are the ones you don't take. Knowing when to stay out is a skill:
whenNot.tip: The market rewards patience. Sitting in cash waiting for a perfect setup IS trading. The best traders spend most of their time doing nothing.

sr.title: Support & Resistance
sr.tip: S/R is the single most important concept in trading. Master this before anything else.

doubleTopBottom.title: Double Top / Double Bottom
doubleTopBottom.tip: Wait for the neckline break with volume confirmation. Many double tops/bottoms fail — the break is the signal, not the second touch.

headShoulders.title: Head & Shoulders
headShoulders.tip: The neckline doesn't have to be perfectly horizontal — it can be slightly angled. Volume should decrease on the right shoulder and spike on the neckline break.

triangles.title: Triangles (Ascending, Descending, Symmetrical)
triangles.tip: Breakouts in the last 25% of the triangle (near the apex) are weaker. The best breakouts happen in the first 50-75% of the pattern.

flags.title: Flags & Pennants
flags.tip: Flags should form quickly (5-15 candles). If consolidation takes too long, the pattern weakens and may not work.

cup.title: Cup & Handle
cup.tip: The handle should NOT drop more than 50% of the cup's depth. If it does, the pattern is weakening.

candlestick.title: Candlestick Basics
candlestick.tip: Single candles are weak signals alone. Always combine with S/R levels and volume. A hammer AT support with high volume = strong. A hammer in the middle of nowhere = meaningless.

glossary.ATH: All-Time High — the highest price ever reached
glossary.ATL: All-Time Low — the lowest price ever recorded
glossary.Candle: A chart bar showing open, high, low, close price for a time period
glossary.DCA: Dollar Cost Averaging — buying fixed amounts at regular intervals
glossary.DeFi: Decentralized Finance — financial services on blockchain without banks
glossary.DYOR: Do Your Own Research — never blindly follow others' advice
glossary.FOMO: Fear Of Missing Out — emotional buying during rallies
glossary.FUD: Fear, Uncertainty, Doubt — negative news/sentiment (sometimes manufactured)
glossary.Gas: Transaction fees paid to blockchain miners/validators
glossary.Halving: Bitcoin event every ~4 years that cuts block rewards in half
glossary.HODL: Hold On for Dear Life — long-term holding strategy
glossary.Leverage: Borrowing money to trade larger positions (amplifies gains AND losses)
glossary.Liquidation: Forced closing of a leveraged position when losses exceed margin
glossary.Long: A trade betting the price will go UP
glossary.MACD: Moving Average Convergence Divergence — trend/momentum indicator
glossary.MarketCap: Total value = Price x Circulating Supply
glossary.MVRV: Market Value to Realized Value — on-chain valuation metric
glossary.OI: Open Interest — total value of open futures/derivatives contracts
glossary.Satoshi: Smallest BTC unit (0.00000001 BTC). Named after creator.
glossary.Short: A trade betting the price will go DOWN
glossary.Slippage: Difference between expected and actual execution price
glossary.StopLoss: Order that auto-sells if price drops to a set level
glossary.Support: Price level where buying pressure prevents further drops
glossary.Resistance: Price level where selling pressure prevents further rises
glossary.TakeProfit: Order that auto-sells when price reaches your profit target
glossary.TVL: Total Value Locked — money deposited in DeFi protocols
glossary.Whale: Entity holding large amounts of crypto (1000+ BTC)
